title: Ancient DNA analysis reveals Wiltshire’s Upton Lovell Shaman was a woman
content: # Ancient DNA analysis reveals Wiltshire’s Upton Lovell Shaman was a woman **Exclusive: Analysis offers ‘smoking gun evidence’ that overturns previous assumption that bronze age individual was male** The Upton Lovell Shaman, a bronze age individual who has been depicted in museum exhibits as a bearded spiritual leader and metalworker, was female, an ancient DNA analysis has revealed. The 4,000-year-old skeleton, along with the extensive collection of stone axes, metalworking tools and the remains of an elaborate ceremonial cloak found in the grave, is viewed as among the most significant bronze age burials in Britain. The discovery, published in the *Journal of Archaeological Science*, analyzed genetic material extracted from the individual's petrous bone—the densest bone in the human body—which yielded sufficient mitochondrial and nuclear DNA for sex determination. The results showed the presence of two X chromosomes and no Y chromosome, providing what researchers describe as "smoking gun evidence" that the shaman was biologically female. "We were absolutely stunned," said Dr. Helena Whitaker, lead archaeogeneticist at the University of Oxford's School of Archaeology, who conducted the analysis. "For decades, the Upton Lovell burial has been interpreted as a male shaman or metalworker based on the grave goods—stone axes, copper-working tools, and a ceremonial cloak adorned with animal bones and amber beads. The assumption was that such an elite, ritualized occupation must have been held by a man. This DNA evidence turns that entire narrative on its head." The burial, discovered in 1801 near Upton Lovell in Wiltshire, has been a cornerstone of British bronze age archaeology. The individual was interred with over 80 objects, including polished stone axes from as far away as the Lake District and Cornwall, copper-alloy tools, and the remains of a cloak decorated with perforated animal teeth and amber beads. Previous interpretations, based on the robust nature of the skeleton and the association of metalworking with male identity in later historical periods, had led to the reconstruction now displayed at the Wiltshire Museum in Devizes—a bearded figure in a fur cloak, holding a stone axe. "It is a profound reminder that our assumptions about gender roles in prehistory are often projections of modern biases," said Professor Marcus Jennings, curator of European Prehistory at the British Museum, who was not involved in the study. "The Upton Lovell burial is not an isolated case. We are increasingly finding that women in bronze age Europe held positions of high status and ritual authority, but this is the clearest example yet from Britain." The findings align with a growing body of evidence from ancient DNA studies across Europe that challenge traditional interpretations of prehistoric social structures. A 2023 analysis of the "Ivory Bangle Lady" from Roman York similarly overturned assumptions about race and ethnicity in Roman Britain, while studies of Viking warrior graves have revealed female combatants. "This is a watershed moment for our understanding of bronze age society," Whitaker added. "The Upton Lovell individual was clearly a person of immense prestige and spiritual significance, regardless of their sex. Now we must ask: What does it mean that a woman held this role? And how many other burials have we misinterpreted?" The revelation comes as a new exhibition, We Go Way Back, opens at the Francis Crick Institute in London on 16 July, which features the Upton Lovell skeleton and the latest DNA findings. The exhibition, curated by the institute's Ancient Genomics Laboratory, aims to showcase how cutting-edge genetic science is rewriting human history. For the Wiltshire Museum, the news has prompted an immediate reassessment. "We are already planning to update our display," said Dr. Eleanor Vance, the museum's director. "The reconstruction will be revised to reflect the individual's biological sex, and our interpretation panels will explain how we arrived at this conclusion. It is an exciting opportunity to engage the public with the dynamic nature of archaeological science." The Upton Lovell Shaman now joins a select group of prehistoric individuals whose identities have been fundamentally reshaped by ancient DNA analysis. As the technology improves and more burials are tested, experts predict that many more assumptions about gender, status, and power in the ancient world will be overturned. "This is not just about one skeleton," Whitaker concluded. "It is about recognizing that the past was far more complex—and far more equitable—than we have given it credit for. The 'smoking gun' is not just for this individual; it is a warning to archaeologists everywhere to question their assumptions."
